content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

June 15, 1995

Daniel M. Kolkey
Legal Affairs Secretary
Governor's Office
Sacramento, California  95814

                                Re:  Your Request for Informal Assistance
                                     Our File No. I-95-134

Dear Mr. Kolkey:

     This is in response to your letter requesting advice
regarding the "gift" provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
"Act").¹  Your question does not involve a particular governmental
decision.  Therefore, we are rendering informal assistance.²

                              QUESTION

     1.  Are legal memoranda provided to the Governor's Office
"informational material" or otherwise exempt from the definition
of "gift" under the Act?

     2.  Are free legal services provided to the Governor's
Office "informational material" or otherwise exempt from the
definition of "gift" under the Act?

                             CONCLUSION

     1.  Pre-existing legal memoranda provided to the Governor's
Office primarily to convey information are considered
"informational material," not gifts.

_______________
1  Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.  Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000-18954.

2  Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code Section 83114; 2 Cal. Code of Regs.  Section
18329(c)(3).)

Our File No. I-95-134
Page 2


     2.     Free legal services provided to the Governor's Office
including the preparation of legal memoranda, generally would not
qualify as "informational material."  Such services could qualify,
however, as gifts to the agency.  Moreover, if such services fall
within the definition of political contributions, they might fall
within the exception for volunteer personal services.

                              FACTS

     You are the Legal Affairs Secretary and Counsel for Governor
Wilson.  Private attorneys have expressed an interest in providing
pro bono legal services to the State through the Governor's
Office.  You wish to make requests for legal memoranda from these
attorneys, containing analyses of issues which come within the
official responsibilities of the Governor's Office.  You would
determine who could best utilize the memoranda.  Some of the firms
in which the attorneys work who would provide the pro bono legal
services are lobbying firms.

                             ANALYSIS

     I.   Gifts

     A "gift" is defined as "any payment to the extent that
consideration of equal or greater value is not received and
includes a rebate or discount in the price of anything of value
unless the rebate or discount is made in the regular course of
business to members of the public without regard to official
status."  (Section 82028(a).)  A "payment" is defined as "a
payment, distribution, transfer, loan, advance, deposit, gift or
other rendering of money, property, services or anything of value,
whether tangible or intangible."  (Section 82044 (emphasis
added).)

     In addition, a lobbyist or lobbying firm cannot make gifts to
public officials lobbied in an amount which aggregates more than
$10 in a calendar month, or to arrange in
the making of any gift, or to arrange for the making of any gift
by any other person.  (Sections 86201 and 86203.)³

     Under Section 82028, anything of value given to a public
official without cost, including legal services, may be a
reportable gift, unless it is expressly exempted from the
definition of "gift."  (Connor Advice Letter, No. A-94-247; Dorsey
Advice Letter, No. I-92-302 (copies enclosed).)

_____________________________
     ³ Moreover, the Act:  (1) limits the acceptance of gifts by state
and local public officials and candidates and the making of gifts
in excess of the gift limits (Sections 89501, 89504, and 89521);
(2) requires reporting of gifts received which total $50 or more
(Section 87207); and (3) may require disqualification with respect
to the donor of the gifts (Section 87103).

Our File No. I-95-134
Page 3


A.  Informational Materials

     You ask in your letter whether legal memoranda provided to
the Governor's Office would qualify as "informational material."
The "informational material" exception in Section 82028(b)
provides that the following items are not gifts:

     (1)  Informational material such as books,
reports, pamphlets, calendars or periodicals.  No
payment for travel or reimbursement for any
expenses shall be deemed "informational material."

     In 1975, the Commission concluded that informational
materials are excluded from the definition of the term "gift" to
ensure that the Act would not inhibit the flow of necessary
information from private sources to government officials.  (In re
Nida (1975) 3 FPPC Ops. 1.)  Regulation 18942.14 further defines
"informational material" to mean items which: 1) serve primarily
to convey information; and, 2) are provided for the purpose of
assisting public officials in the performance of their official
duties.

     1.  Tangible Items

     We have advised that a tangible item, such as a magazine,
that provides information relevant to governmental business is
"informational material."  (McCann Advice Letter, No. A-92-485.)
Similarly, if a person gives to your office a previously prepared
legal memorandum or brief from their files, those materials
normally would be informational.  However, this would not
encompass the services of an attorney, for example, who agreed to
research and prepare a memorandum for your office.  Such services
would constitute a gift as discussed below.  We conclude that if a
person provides copies of memoranda or other documents in their
files for the purpose of conveying information relevant to a
governmental issue, we would consider the documents to be
"informational material."

     2.  Intangible Items

     In applying the "informational material" exception, we have
also applied the exception, under certain circumstances, to
intangible items provided to specific public officials, such as an
informational tour.  (In re Speliman, 1 FPPC Ops. 16; Regulation
18942.1(c).)  In applying the exception to intangible items,
however, we have consistently advised that the free informational
materials must be solely informational.  (Rafuse Advice Letter,
No. I-91-232.)  The issue you present is whether free legal

_______________
4  The Commission adopted amendments to this regulation on
May 4, 1995.  However, those changes do not affect our
conclusions.

Our File No. I-95-134
Page 4


services requested by you, and which may assist you in the
performance of your official duties, are analogous.

According to the facts provided, private attorneys have
expressed an interest in providing pro bono legal services to the
Governor's Office concerning legal issues which come within the
official responsibilities of the office. As Legal Affairs
Secretary for the Governor, you would be making specific requests
and determining who could best utilize the memoranda. The
services rendered would not serve primarily to convey information
from private sources; rather, they would serve primarily to
supplement the work of the Governor's Office legal staff in
connection with various governmental projects and litigation.
Moreover, the services will involve reaching legal conclusions,
rather than merely facilitating the flow of information.
Therefore, the "informational material" exception would rarely, if
ever, apply to free legal services provided by third parties to
the Governor's office.

B. Gift to an Agency

Even if a gift were to result, however, Regulation 18944.2
may apply. This regulation sets forth criteria for determining
whether a gift used by a public official is a gift to an agency,
rather than to the public official who benefits from or uses the
gift.

Regulation 18944.2(a) provides:

A payment, which is a gift as defined in
Government Code Section 82028, shall be deemed a
gift to a public agency, and not a gift to a public
official, if all of the following requirements are
met:

(1) The agency receives and controls the
payment.

(2) The payment is used for official
agency business.

(3) The agency, in its sole discretion,
determines the specific official or officials
who shall use the payment. However, the donor
may identify a specific purpose for the
agency's use of the payment, so long as the
donor does not designate the specific official
or officials who may use the payment.

(4) The agency memorializes the payment
in a written public record which embodies the
requirements of subdivisions (a)(1) to (a)(3)
of this regulation set forth above and which:

Our File No. I-95-134
Page 5


(A) Identifies the donor and the
official, officials, or class of
officials receiving or using the
payment;

(B) Describes the official agency
use and the nature and amount of the
payment; and

(C) Is filed with the agency
official who maintains the records of the
agency's statements of economic interests
where the agency has a specific office
for the maintenance of such statements,
or where no specific office exists for
the maintenance of such statements, at a
designated office of the agency, and the
filing is done within 30 days of the
receipt of the payment by the agency.

You have indicated that the legal services would be provided
to the Governor's Office. The Governor's Office is an agency
which may receive and control the payment. (See Regulation
18944.1(a)(1).) In addition, the legal services, presumably,
would be provided for official agency business within the meaning
of Regulation 18944.2(a)(2).)

You serve as the Governor's Legal Affairs Secretary. The
services to be provided concern work under your area of
responsibility. You also state that you would make the requests
for legal advice and determine who would ultimately use the
services. Thus, although the ultimate beneficiary may be another
public official or a state agency, for example, you would
determine who would ultimately use the legal services.

Subdivision (a)(3) of Regulation 18944.2 requires that the
agency in its sole discretion determine the specific official or
officials who shall use the gift. Determining whether this
requirement is met, where an agency official who requests a
service and who may benefit from that service, may accept that
gift on behalf of his or her agency is problematic.

In the Delventhal Advice Letter, No. A-94-207, we advised
that if a public official who is the intended recipient of a gift
accepts the gift on behalf of his or her agency, the official has
received a reportable gift. Thus, it would appear that as the
Legal Affairs Secretary for the Governor, you may be the intended
recipient of legal services, and the requirements of subdivision
(a)(3) of Regulation 18944.2 would not be met if you accept the
gift. However, we recognize that this requirement of the
regulation would never be met if a public official could never
make a recommendation to his or her agency about the acceptance of
a gift which concerns his or her area of responsibility. Thus,
provided that you, or any intended recipient of the services, do

Our File No. I-95-134
Page 6


not make the ultimate decision concerning the acceptance of the
gift by your agency, and no unusual personal benefit to you would
result, this requirement would be met.

     Finally, you state in your letter that you will memorialize
the receipt of the memoranda (presumably the legal services
requested).  Please note that the requirement that the
information be filed with the agency official who maintains the
records of the agency's statements of economic interests where the
agency has a specific office for the maintenance of such
statements.  Where no specific office exists for the maintenance
of such statements, the information must be filed at an office
within the agency which has been designated for the filing of the
information.

     Thus, if the requirements of the regulation are met, we would
not treat the donation of free legal services as gifts to any
individual official of the Governor's Office.

II.  Contributions

     Also, please note that the term "gift" does not include
campaign contributions required to be reported under the
provisions of the Act.  (Section 82028(b)(4).)  Section 82015 and
Regulation 18215 define "contribution" to include any payment for
which full and adequate consideration is not made to the donor,
which is received by or made at the behest of an elected
officeholder, unless it is clear that the payment was made for
personal purposes unrelated to his or her candidacy or status as
an officeholder.⁵  Therefore, legal services received by or made
at the behest of the Governor could be deemed to be a contribution
rather than a gift.

     However, Section 82015 and Regulation 18215(e) further
provide that the term "contribution" does not include "volunteer
personal services."  Thus, for example, if an attorney wishes to
donate his or her legal services (on noncompensated time) in
connection with litigation that arises during the course of the
Governor's official duties, no reportable contribution to the
Governor would result.

     You state that the attorneys who have volunteered to provide
the services may be employed by lobbyist firms.  Please note, that
the exemption for "volunteer personal services" does not apply if:
(1) a law firm pays an attorney to render the personal services
for political purposes, and (2) the attorney spends more than 10
percent of his or her compensated time performing such services in
any one month.  (Regulation 18423; Keller Advice Letter, No. A-92-
305.)  Pursuant to Regulation 18423(b), personal services are
rendered for political purposes if they are carried on for the

_____________
5     Commission staff is currently considering submitting amendments
to this regulation which may significantly alter the regulation.

Our File No. I-95-134
Page 7


purpose of influencing or attempting to influence the action of
the voters for or against the nomination or election of one or
more candidates. Therefore, if those were the facts, the Governor
would have received a non-monetary contribution from the
attorney's law firm.

III. Activity Expenses

Also, please note that if a payment is incurred, made by, or
arranged by a lobbyist or lobbying firm in connection with legal
services rendered, the payment may constitute a reportable
activity expenses where it benefits in whole or in part any
legislative official, elective state office, elective state
candidate, or a member of the immediate family of one of
these individuals. Activity expenses include gifts, consulting
fees, salaries, and any other form of compensation. (Section
86111; Regulations 18611 and 18616.)

If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

[signature]
Luisa Menchaca
Counsel, Legal Division

Enclosures

QUESTION: 1.
Are legal memoranda provided to the Governor’s Office
“informational material” or otherwise exempt from the definition
of “gift” under the Act?
2.
Are free legal services provided to the Governor’s
Office “informational material” or otherwise exempt from the
definition of”gift” under the Act?

CONCLUSION: 1.
Pre—existing legal memoranda provided to the Governor’s
Office primarily to convey information are considered
“informational material,” not gifts.
-
;
2 Cal.
Code of Regs.
Section
18329(c) (3).)


Our File No.
1—95—134
2.
Free legal services provided to the Governor’s Office
including the preparation of legal memoranda,
generally would not
qualify as “informational material.”
Such services could qualify,
however,
as gifts to the agency.
Moreover, if such services fall
within the definition of political contributions,
they might fall
within the exception for volunteer personal services.

Kolkey Legal Affairs Secretary Governor's Office Sacramento, California 95814 Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No. I-95-134 Dear Mr. Kolkey: This is in response to your letter requesting advice regarding the "gift" provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Your question does not involve a particular governmental decision. Therefore, we are rendering informal assistance.² QUESTION 1. Are legal memoranda provided to the Governor's Office "informational material" or otherwise exempt from the definition of "gift" under the Act? 2. Are free legal services provided to the Governor's Office "informational material" or otherwise exempt from the definition of "gift" under the Act? CONCLUSION 1. Pre-existing legal memoranda provided to the Governor's Office primarily to convey information are considered "informational material," not gifts. _______________ 1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. Free legal services provided to the Governor's Office including the preparation of legal memoranda, generally would not qualify as "informational material." Such services could qualify, however, as gifts to the agency. Moreover, if such services fall within the definition of political contributions, they might fall within the exception for volunteer personal services. FACTS You are the Legal Affairs Secretary and Counsel for Governor Wilson. Private attorneys have expressed an interest in providing pro bono legal services to the State through the Governor's Office. You wish to make requests for legal memoranda from these attorneys, containing analyses of issues which come within the official responsibilities of the Governor's Office. You would determine who could best utilize the memoranda. Some of the firms in which the attorneys work who would provide the pro bono legal services are lobbying firms. ANALYSIS I. Gifts A "gift" is defined as "any payment to the extent that consideration of equal or greater value is not received and includes a rebate or discount in the price of anything of value unless the rebate or discount is made in the regular course of business to members of the public without regard to official status." (Section 82028(a).) A "payment" is defined as "a payment, distribution, transfer, loan, advance, deposit, gift or other rendering of money, property, services or anything of value, whether tangible or intangible." (Section 82044 (emphasis added).) In addition, a lobbyist or lobbying firm cannot make gifts to public officials lobbied in an amount which aggregates more than $10 in a calendar month, or to arrange in the making of any gift, or to arrange for the making of any gift by any other person. (Sections 86201 and 86203.)³ Under Section 82028, anything of value given to a public official without cost, including legal services, may be
